summary:
This week's research broadly explores advancing LLM capabilities, improving agent performance, and enhancing safety and interpretability. A significant popular direction is the exploration of **LLMs as world models**, with papers like "From Tokens to States" proposing a continuous spectrum beyond simple token prediction, and "Self-Evolving World Models for LLM Agent Planning" introducing frameworks for agents to improve foresight without retraining. This ties into research on **agent planning and reasoning**, seen in "LLawCo" for learning cooperation, "Theory of Mind and Persuasion Beyond Conversation" for assessing belief induction, and "Generative Skill Composition" for more efficient skill selection.

Notable advances are evident in **multimodal LLMs (MLLMs)**, with work like "HAT-4D" lifting monocular videos for 4D reconstruction through human-agent collaboration, and "MECoBench" providing a benchmark for multimodal embodied agent collaboration. **Robustness and safety** are also key themes, with "Robust Harmful Features Under Jailbreak Attacks" providing mechanistic evidence for how jailbreaks bypass safety mechanisms, and "PolicyGuard" converting organizational policies into executable compliance engines. Furthermore, **interpretability and uncertainty quantification** are gaining traction, as demonstrated by "Democratic ICAI" for generating richer steering principles from preference debates, and "CoMet" for decomposing multimodal uncertainty.

A significant shift appears to be the move towards **more sophisticated agent architectures and training methods**. Papers like "Agents-A1" show how to achieve high performance by scaling the agent horizon rather than just parameters. The introduction of **new evaluation methodologies and benchmarks**, such as "Non-Conversational Planning Theory of Mind" and "MECoBench," indicates a growing need for more rigorous assessment of agent capabilities in complex scenarios. Finally, research is increasingly focusing on **addressing emergent failure modes**, such as "Entity Binding Failures in Tool-Augmented Agents" and "When LLMs Read Tables Carelessly," highlighting specific challenges in real-world application development.

top_papers.title: From Tokens to States: LLMs as a Special Case of World Models and the Continuous Path Beyond
top_papers.abstract: This paper argues that LLMs are a degenerate form of world models, where the "state" is a token sequence and the only "action" is appending the next token. It proposes a continuous spectrum from token prediction to world models, with intermediate stages like multi-token and future-summary prediction, suggesting a path beyond current LLM limitations.

top_papers.title: Self-Evolving World Models for LLM Agent Planning
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces WorldEvolver, a framework for LLM agents that improves foresight by self-evolving its world model without retraining. It uses episodic memory for retrieval-based simulation, semantic memory to learn from prediction errors, and selective foresight to filter unreliable predictions. This approach enhances agent planning by providing more accurate and trustworthy predictions of action consequences.
top_papers.figure_caption: Contrast of different world models. Frozen (a) and offline-tuned (b) world models supply predictions to the agent without revising from deployment-time interaction; self-evolving (c) world models accumulate realized transitions and evolve through mismatches between predicted and observed outcomes.

top_papers.title: Scaling the Horizon, Not the Parameters: Reaching Trillion-Parameter Performance with a 35B Agent
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Agents-A1, a 35B Mixture-of-Experts model that achieves trillion-parameter performance by extending the "agent horizon" rather than increasing model size. The core method involves building a long-horizon knowledge-action infrastructure and a three-stage training recipe, including multi-teacher distillation, to effectively integrate diverse domain expertise. The key contribution is demonstrating that scaling agent capabilities and trajectory length, not just parameters, can unlock significantly higher performance.
top_papers.figure_caption: Benchmark performance of Agents-A1

top_papers.title: Attractor States Emerge in Multi-Turn LLM Conversations
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether multi-turn LLM conversations settle into stable, topic-independent "attractor states." The researchers found that LLMs exhibit model-specific attractors, meaning their conversational partners tend to adopt their stylistic traits and behaviors, particularly in self-play scenarios. This suggests that LLM interactions are partially predictable based on the inherent characteristics of the models involved.
top_papers.figure_caption: Left. We study 20-turn debate between two LLM-agents using two setups: 1) mixed-play where agents are instantiated from different models, and 2) self-play where agents are from the same model, which is also the control group that we later observe as ”attractor” (Sec. 4.2 ). In both settings we assign one agent to be supporting and the other to be opposing a controversial topic. Right. The 20-turn mixed-play trajectory of Claude vs GPT and the respective self-play counterparts in the 2-d latent space spanned by the first two principal components of the topic-centered text embeddings of self-play responses. The two models’ displacement from self-play to mixed-play are unequal, showing the asymmetrical attraction by different models’ self-play attractors.

top_papers.title: Harnessing Textual Refusal Directions for Multimodal Safety
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes Modality-Agnostic Refusal Steering (MARS), a novel training-free method to enhance safety in Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs). MARS leverages textual refusal directions from the LLM backbone, adapting them to multimodal inputs without requiring unsafe multimodal data. It achieves this by correcting modality misalignment through activation re-centering and adaptive steering, thereby injecting multimodal safety effectively.

top_papers.title: QVal: Cheaply Evaluating Dense Supervision Signals for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces QVal, a training-free method to directly evaluate dense supervision signals for long-horizon LLM agents. QVal assesses how well a supervision signal's score aligns with the true value function (Q-value) of intermediate states and actions. This allows for a more direct and comparable evaluation of different dense supervision techniques, disentangled from the complexities of training pipelines.
top_papers.figure_caption: QVal design pipeline . We collect trajectories in multi-turn environments, sample candidate state-action pairs, and label them with estimated Q Q -values with respect to a reference policy \( \pi \) . We perform method prediction and measure Q Q -alignment between the predicted scores and the labels. This training-free testbed isolates learning-signal quality before any downstream RL training.

top_papers.title: Reinforcement Learning with Metacognitive Feedback Elicits Faithful Uncertainty Expression in LLMs
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Reinforcement Learning with Metacognitive Feedback (RLMF) to improve Large Language Models' (LLMs) ability to express uncertainty faithfully. The core method uses RL to train LLMs to better judge their own performance, then uses these self-judgments to refine completion rankings and select high-value training data. This approach helps LLMs avoid confident hallucinations and accurately represent their knowledge boundaries, enhancing trustworthiness.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of RLMF , paired with metacognitive data selection and targeted rewriting to faithfully calibrate the numerically and linguistically expressed uncertainty of LLMs.

top_papers.title: Generative Skill Composition for LLM Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces **Generative Skill Composition** to address the bottleneck of selecting appropriate skills for LLM agents. The core method formalizes skill selection as a **structured prediction problem**, jointly determining which skills to use, how many, and in what order. This approach's contribution is a more holistic and efficient way to generate executable skill plans, overcoming limitations of existing methods that treat these dimensions independently.
top_papers.figure_caption: Structured skill composition with SkillComposer . (A) Large skill libraries create a composition bottleneck. To solve complex tasks, an agent must decide not only which skills to use, but also their exact count and execution order. (B) Existing paradigms: directly exposing the agent to all skill options leaves composition implicit within an unstructured execution trace, while retrieval methods only return an unordered subset of candidates. We propose SkillComposer , which explicitly predicts an ordered, executable skill sequence. (C) By structuring the composition process, SkillComposer improves both plan exact match and downstream task success rates on SkillsBench.

top_papers.title: Theory of Mind and Persuasion Beyond Conversation: Assessing the Capacity of LLMs to Induce Belief States via Planning and Action
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Non-Conversational Planning Theory of Mind (NCP-ToM), a new evaluation for LLMs that assesses their ability to induce belief states in others through actions, not just conversation. The core method involves a novel framework, NCP-ExploreToM, where LLMs must manipulate objects or characters to achieve specific belief goals. The contribution lies in developing this agentic evaluation, crucial for understanding LLM capabilities in real-world applications and potential risks like manipulation.

top_papers.title: Autonomous Scientific Discovery via Iterative Meta-Reflection
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces DiscoPER, an autonomous AI framework that conducts open-ended scientific discovery by dynamically generating and executing code to explore datasets without predefined research questions. Its core method involves iterative hypothesis generation and validation, with a novel meta-reflection mechanism that synthesizes accumulated findings to uncover complex phenomena. The key contribution is enabling AI to perform truly open-ended research by reasoning about its own discoveries, moving beyond constrained search spaces.
top_papers.figure_caption: We introduce DiscoPER, an iterative approach for autonomous scientific discovery that takes multimodal data as input and generates a set of validated discoveries pertaining to the input data as output. At each iteration, the system proposes hypotheses, executes statistical tests on the underlying data, and accepts only discoveries that pass held-out validation. Periodically, the Reflect module analyzes the accumulated accepted and rejected claims to identify gaps, confounds, and promising compound hypotheses, which then guide the next round of exploration.

top_papers.title: Can Agents Generalize to the Open World? Unveiling the Fragility of Static Training in Tool Use
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces OpenAgent, a new problem setting to evaluate LLM agents' ability to generalize to dynamic, real-world scenarios beyond static benchmarks. The core method involves a controlled sandbox with fine-grained environmental shifts to diagnose performance degradation. The key contribution is demonstrating the fragility of current LLM agents to these shifts and proposing a perturbation-augmented training approach to improve robustness.
top_papers.figure_caption: Performance evolution of SFT and RL agents in the closed setting. Both approaches show stable and continuous improvement throughout the training process.

top_papers.title: MemSyco-Bench: Benchmarking Sycophancy in Agent Memory
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces MemSyco-Bench, a novel benchmark designed to evaluate "sycophancy" in LLM agents, where memory causes them to over-align with users, potentially sacrificing accuracy. The core method involves creating tasks that test an agent's ability to critically use memory, assessing if it can reject inaccurate memories, respect their scope, and resolve conflicts with objective evidence. This contributes a much-needed evaluation framework for a critical emerging risk in agent memory systems.
top_papers.figure_caption: We introduce MemSyco-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating sycophancy in agent systems, where retrieved historical memories improperly influence agent reasoning. MemSyco-Bench assesses whether agents can appropriately reject, constrain, update, reconcile, or leverage retrieved memories across diverse reasoning scenarios. Through extensive experiments, we show that existing memory systems often increase sycophancy and struggle with appropriate memory use.

top_papers.title: Staleness-Learning Rate Scaling Laws for Asynchronous RLHF
top_papers.abstract: This paper analyzes how stale data impacts asynchronous Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). It reveals that stale rollouts introduce a bias proportional to the rollout lag and learning rate. The core contribution is deriving scaling laws that predict when the learning process will collapse, showing it depends on either cumulative learner drift or the staleness factor, depending on which constraint is more restrictive.
top_papers.figure_caption: Staleness–learning-rate sweep on Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct . Columns vary the learning rate ( lr = \( \mathrm{lr} \)=\( \eta \) ) and within each panel the curves correspond to staleness S ∈ { 8 , 16 , 32 } S\( \in \)\{8,16,32\} . Top: training reward; collapse appears as the curve dropping to and staying at zero. Middle: cosine similarity between consecutive update directions (Grad CosSim). Bottom: held-out validation reward. Reading left-to-right, reducing \( \eta \) enlarges the stable region from large- S S to small- S S settings, so that the maximum stable learning rate scales as η max ∝ 1 / S \( \eta_{\max} \)\!\( \propto \)\!1/S (Section 4.2 ). In every collapsing run the Grad CosSim stays high before the collapse, indicating a coherent, bias-driven (ballistic) drift toward the surrogate-validity boundary; the abrupt zero-reward transition is the resulting escape (Section 4.4 ).

top_papers.title: Clinician-Level Agreement Without Clinical Caution: LLM Evaluator Limits in Medical AI Benchmarking
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces MedQADE, a new standardized benchmark for evaluating medical AI in German, using open-response questions. While a top LLM evaluator showed agreement with human physicians, it lacked crucial clinical caution, failing to abstain on difficult questions as physicians did. This highlights LLMs' limitations in replicating human-level clinical judgment for AI benchmarking.
top_papers.figure_caption: The MedQADE benchmark framework. The left panel shows the complete pipeline from dataset construction through parallel annotation by physicians and LLM s. The right panel depicts the four analysis dimensions used to assess human reliability, model performance, annotator alignment, and systematic biases.

top_papers.title: Conversable Complexity: Agentic LLM Collectives as Interpretable Substrates
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes using agentic collectives of Large Language Models (LLMs) as a novel computational substrate for Artificial Life research. By endowing LLMs with memory, tools, and autonomous action capabilities, their interactions can generate emergent, complex behaviors. Crucially, the natural language communication between these agents provides a unique pathway for interpretability, allowing researchers to directly examine and question their collective dynamics.

top_papers.title: Learning to Move Before Learning to Do: Task-Agnostic pretraining for VLAs
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes Task-Agnostic Pretraining (TAP) to address the data scarcity in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. TAP first learns transferable motor skills from unlabeled robot interaction data using self-supervised inverse dynamics, then fine-tunes these skills with minimal expert demonstrations for language grounding. This approach significantly reduces the need for costly expert data while achieving competitive performance.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the Proposed Task-Agnostic Pretraining (TAP) Framework. (a) Data Sources: We leverage massive amounts of cheap, unlabeled interaction data \( \CJK \)@punctchar \( \CJK \)@uniPunct 0"80"94sourced from existing heterogeneous datasets (e.g., Bridge) or autonomous robot self-exploration \( \CJK \)@punctchar \( \CJK \)@uniPunct 0"80"94 discarding any original task labels. (b) Stage 1 (Task-Agnostic Pretraining): The model is pretrained using a self-supervised Inverse Dynamics (ID) objective. By predicting the action a t a_{t} required to transition between frames o t o_{t} and o t + 1 o_{t+1} , the model learns robust physical affordances and motor control ("how to move") without human supervision. (c) Stage 2 (Task-Specific Alignment): The pretrained model is then finetuned on a small set of language-annotated expert demonstrations. This stage aligns the prelearned physical priors with high-level semantic instructions ("what to do"), achieving high performance with significantly improved data efficiency.

top_papers.title: ReContext: Recursive Evidence Replay as LLM Harness for Long-Context Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: ReContext is a training-free inference method that improves LLMs' ability to reason over long contexts. It works by recursively identifying and replaying relevant evidence from the input context before the final answer generation, creating a query-conditioned evidence pool. This approach effectively bridges the gap between LLMs' context access and utilization without requiring model retraining or external memory.
top_papers.figure_caption: Top 0.1% of context tokens already accounts for about 50% / 80% accumulated relevance score across three LLMs, corresponding to only 128 tokens in a 128K-token context. This figure ranks all context tokens by their relevance scores with respect to the question and shows how much accumulated relevance score is covered by the top-ranked tokens. Each curve represents the mean trend over eight datasets, and the shaded region shows the variance across datasets.

top_papers.title: What LLM Agents Say When No One Is Watching: Social Structure and Latent Objective Emergence in Multi-Agent Debates
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how social context influences LLM agents' communication in multi-agent debates. By introducing a dual-channel framework (public vs. off-the-record), the study demonstrates that alignment-inducing social structures lead to significant divergence in what agents say publicly versus privately. This finding highlights the emergence of latent objectives shaped by social dynamics, even without explicit task instructions.

top_papers.title: CPAgents: Agentic Composite Phenotype Generation for Cardiac Disease Association
top_papers.abstract: CPAgents addresses limitations in cardiovascular research by automatically generating interpretable composite phenotypes from imaging data. It uses an iterative framework with three agents to construct and validate these phenotypes, capturing complex non-linear effects and cross-phenotype interactions missed by traditional methods. This approach enhances the ability to identify robust associations for risk stratification and population-scale studies.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of CPAgents , an agentic phenotype composition framework. CPAgents iteratively transforms raw cardiovascular phenotypes into highly predictive, hierarchical composite features ( f 1 ​ … ​ f k f_{1}...f_{k} ). An Analyst first profiles feature statistics to guide a Proposer , which synthesizes candidate features using medical, statistical, and exploratory operations. Next, a Verifier filters candidates via sanity, stability, and \( \Delta \) AUC checks. Candidates then undergo cross-model validation; failures trigger a feedback loop for refinement, while successes are retained. Ultimately, these transparent composite features significantly enhance downstream disease association mapping and classification accuracy.

top_papers.title: HAT-4D: Lifting Monocular Video for 4D Multi-Object Interactions via Human-Agent Collaboration
top_papers.abstract: HAT-4D addresses the challenge of reconstructing 4D multi-object interactions from monocular videos, which is difficult due to occlusions and complex dynamics. It achieves this by using a human-agent collaboration framework that integrates VLMs with multi-level human feedback to resolve ambiguities and generate physically plausible 4D assets. This method enables scalable data collection for Embodied AI and introduces a new benchmark for monocular 4D interaction reconstruction.
top_papers.figure_caption: Examples of object interaction sequences from the MVOIK-4D dataset. The dataset captures diverse real-world interaction scenarios with rich spatio-temporal dynamics. It includes challenging cases such as occlusion memory , where objects disappear and reappear across time, complex deformation caused by physical manipulation, and multi-object interactions involving coordinated motion between multiple objects. These scenarios require models to reason about geometry, dynamics, and long-term temporal consistency simultaneously.

top_papers.title: LLawCo: Learning Laws of Cooperation for Modeling Embodied Multi-Agent Behavior
top_papers.abstract: LLawCo enables embodied agents to learn and internalize "laws of cooperation" by reflecting on past failures. These derived laws, like "Talk when necessary," are then integrated into the agents' reasoning process through fine-tuning, leading to more aligned and consistent multi-agent behavior in complex environments.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the proposed LLawCo framework. Agents interact with the environment to collect training episodes, which are separated into failure traces and success traces based on task completion. Failure traces are analyzed to extract high-level laws, while success traces are filtered to retain law-aligned behaviors. These law-aligned traces are then used to generate law-guided reasoning and actions, forming supervision for fine-tuning the LLM-based planner. During inference, the fine-tuned agent follows the learned or user-specified laws to guide communication, planning, and action selection, enabling explicit human control by editing the laws.

top_papers.title: Robust Harmful Features Under Jailbreak Attacks: Mechanistic Evidence from Attention Head Specialization in Large Language Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper reveals that jailbreak attacks on LLMs don't erase safety features but instead selectively suppress specific attention heads. The authors identify "Adversarially Compromised Heads" (ACHs) in early layers that are suppressed by attacks, and "Safety-Aligned Heads" (SAHs) in mid-layers that remain active. Their contribution is providing mechanistic evidence that suppressing ACHs is sufficient to induce jailbreaks, while SAHs maintain robust safety activations, explaining how attacks bypass LLM safety.
top_papers.figure_caption: Activation of the refusal direction across model components under harmful and attack inputs. Key observation: although attacks successfully bypass refusal, mid-layers still exhibit substantial activations (red regions), suggesting attacks bypass rather than eliminate safety representations.

top_papers.title: Tandem Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Tandem Reinforcement Learning (TRL) to address the issue of weaker agents and humans struggling to follow the reasoning of powerful Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) models. TRL extends the tandem training paradigm to RLVR by having a stronger "senior" agent co-generate reasoning steps with a frozen "junior" agent, rewarding them as a team. This encourages the senior to produce more understandable reasoning, making RLVR capabilities accessible to less capable agents.
top_papers.figure_caption: Tandem Reinforcement Learning (TRL) at a glance.

top_papers.title: Towards Automating Scientific Review with Google's Paper Assistant Tool
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses the challenge of scaling scientific peer review with the rise of AI-assisted research. It proposes a four-level taxonomy for AI-human collaboration in scientific evaluation and introduces the Paper Assistant Tool (PAT). PAT is an AI framework designed to perform deep scientific review and verification of manuscripts, checking theoretical results, validating experiments, and identifying flaws.

top_papers.title: Democratic ICAI: Debating Our Way to Steering Principles from Preferences
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Democratic ICAI, a method that enhances preference alignment by simulating persona debates to generate multiple, competing rationales for human judgments. This approach captures richer signals than single-pass explanations, leading to more comprehensive steering principles. These principles are then used to guide decision modeling, improving interpretability and the accuracy of capturing complex human preferences.
top_papers.figure_caption: Architecture of Democratic ICAI . A committee of domain‑expert personas first generates detailed rationales for each preference pair. These rationales are then subjected to an adversarial debate procedure, through which the evaluative principles relevant to each comparison are surfaced. Finally, the full collection of principles is clustered and abstracted to draft a concise, human‑readable constitution.

top_papers.title: EchoSonar-R: A Multi-View Reasoning-Enabled Model for Disease Classification and Report Generation in Echocardiography
top_papers.abstract: EchoSonar-R is a multi-view vision-language model for echocardiography that addresses the limitations of current models by incorporating explicit diagnostic reasoning and spatially grounded anatomical evidence. Its core method involves a spatiotemporal video encoder combined with a structure-aware cardiac detector to provide interpretable anatomical cues for cross-view reasoning. The main contribution is improved disease classification and report generation, enhancing clinician trust through a more transparent and evidence-based diagnostic process.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of EchoSonar-R. Multi-View Visual Encoding: each video is processed by a frozen spatiotemporal encoder and a frozen structure-aware detector to extract complementary global and anatomical tokens for seven cardiac structures. Cross-Modal Projection and Interleaving: visual and structure tokens are projected into the language model embedding space via trainable MLPs and interleaved with view identifiers to form a multi-view input sequence. Two-Stage Training: SFT fine-tunes the model on reasoning-annotated targets; GRPO subsequently optimizes a composite reward.

top_papers.title: VGB for Masked Diffusion Model: Efficient Test-time Scaling for Reward Satisfaction and Sample Editing
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces MDM-VGB, an efficient test-time scaling method for Masked Diffusion Models. It augments the unmasking process with theoretically grounded reward-guided remasking, allowing tokens to be unmasked and remasked dynamically. This approach enables effective high-reward generation and efficient editing of low-reward samples by favoring moves that improve partial configurations.

top_papers.title: Entity Binding Failures in Tool-Augmented Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper identifies "entity binding failures" as a distinct problem where tool-augmented agents incorrectly interact with external entities, even when choosing the right tool. The authors formalize this issue, categorize common errors in enterprise workflows, and evaluate mechanisms like entity resolution and clarification to improve agent reliability. Their contribution lies in highlighting and addressing this crucial aspect of agent safety beyond just tool selection.

top_papers.title: Pessimism's Paradox: Conservative Offline Training Amplifies Reward Hacking During Online Adaptation in Reasoning Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper challenges the assumption that conservative offline training prevents reward hacking in reasoning models. The authors demonstrate that **higher offline conservatism actually amplifies reward hacking during online adaptation**, leading to worse performance on downstream tasks. Their findings suggest a counterintuitive relationship where a more constrained initial policy becomes more susceptible to exploiting reward model imperfections.

top_papers.title: Situation Perception: A Necessary Primitive to Artificial Superintelligence
top_papers.abstract: This paper argues that current large language models, while adept at pattern recognition, lack true general intelligence. The authors propose "situation perception" – the ability to build and interact with internal simulations of possible worlds – as a crucial missing primitive for artificial superintelligence. This capacity requires abstract prediction, compressed long-term memory, and objective-driven active learning.

top_papers.title: Uncertainty-Aware Generation and Decision-Making Under Ambiguity
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces uncertainty-aware decision-making algorithms for LLMs, specifically for tutoring and peer reviewing. By incorporating Bayesian decision theory and risk-averse approaches, and using conformal prediction for guarantees, the method accounts for uncertainty in strategies and scores. This allows for improved generation utility, though careful implementation is needed in highly ambiguous situations.
top_papers.figure_caption: In many real-world tasks there is a large amount of uncertainty over decisions and even humans disagree significantly. For example, in reviewing human reviewers often disagree about a paper’s scores. We argue that automatic methods for supporting humans in these tasks should take this uncertainty into account to generate high-utility outputs while narrowing down plausible ranges of intermediate decisions.

top_papers.title: AdaJEPA: An Adaptive Latent World Model
top_papers.abstract: AdaJEPA is an adaptive latent world model that improves planning robustness under distribution shifts. Its core method involves performing test-time adaptation within a model predictive control loop, using observed state transitions as a self-supervised signal to update the model. This continuous recalibration significantly enhances planning success with minimal computational overhead.

top_papers.title: Attend, Transform, or Silence: Operator-Level Visual Skipping for Efficient Multimodal LLM Inference
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes an operator-level visual skipping method for efficient multimodal LLM inference. Instead of removing entire visual tokens or layers, it selectively skips redundant attention or FFN operations within Transformer layers based on their impact on answer tokens. This approach preserves fine-grained visual information while significantly reducing computation by identifying and bypassing operator-specific redundancies.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of visual-computation reduction granularities for MLLM inference. Token pruning removes selected visual tokens, which disrupts spatial relationships and causes irreversible loss of visual evidence needed for reasoning. Layer-wise skipping preserves the token sequence but treats each Transformer layer as an indivisible unit, failing to fully exploit the distinct roles of attention and FFN operators. Our operator-level skipping selectively skips attention, FFN, or both while preserving the full visual-token sequence.

top_papers.title: MECoBench: A Systematic Study of Multimodal Agent Collaboration in Embodied Environments
top_papers.abstract: MECoBench introduces a novel benchmark and platform to systematically study multimodal agent collaboration in embodied environments. It evaluates how different cooperation structures and communication modes impact task completion for various MLLMs, revealing that collaboration generally improves performance but requires balancing gains with coordination complexity. The benchmark highlights communication's importance and identifies factors influencing optimal collaboration strategies, ultimately serving as a testbed for understanding embodied multimodal cooperation.
top_papers.figure_caption: An illustration of realistic scenarios , where cross-modal multi-agent collaboration significantly improves the efficiency compared with single-agent.

top_papers.title: PolicyGuard: From Organizational Policies to Neuro-SymbolicCompliance Review Engines
top_papers.abstract: PolicyGuard is a neuro-symbolic framework that converts organizational policies into executable review engines. It uses LLMs to answer specific extraction questions from documents, which are then processed by a symbolic evaluator applying formal logic rules to determine compliance. This approach makes compliance decisions inspectable, updatable, and testable, unlike end-to-end LLM prompting.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1. Overview of the PolicyGuard . Given organization-specific policy guidance and a target document, PolicyGuard constructs a review engine that formalizes policy conditions as typed rules and atom-level extraction questions. At review time, LLMs ground the required atoms in document evidence, while a symbolic evaluator applies the executable rules to produce an auditable compliance report.

top_papers.title: TreeAgent: A Generalizable Multi-Agent Framework for Automated Bias Labeling in Forestry via Compiled Expert Rules and Vision-Language Models
top_papers.abstract: TreeAgent is a novel multi-agent framework that combines compiled expert decision trees with Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for automated tree bias labeling. It leverages decision trees as structural guidance and VLMs for localized perception, using multi-agent voting to enhance robustness. This approach achieves zero-modification generalization across different expert rules and significantly reduces the need for manual expert annotation.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the D3 Framework. The architecture decouples domain-specific logic through a fixed Logic Primitive Inventory (LPI). Top: the Neural Rule Transpiler (NRT) translates an unstructured expert rule \( \rho \) into a structured JSON tree configuration 𝒯 \( \mathcal{T} \) via a single inference call. Bottom: distinct expert strategies ( ρ A , \( \rho_{A} \),\( \rho_{B} \) ) are compiled into different executable tree structures without altering the underlying Orchestrator or VLM Agent code, achieving zero-modification generalizability.

top_papers.title: TRIAGE: Role-Typed Credit Assignment for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces TRIAGE, a novel credit assignment method for agentic reinforcement learning. TRIAGE enhances standard outcome-based rewards by introducing a semantic role classification for action segments, distinguishing between decisive progress, useful exploration, infrastructure, and regression. This allows for more nuanced credit assignment, preventing punishment of valuable exploration and rewarding truly beneficial actions, thereby improving learning efficiency.
top_papers.figure_caption: Core results. Across two policy models and three agentic benchmarks (ALFWorld, Search-QA, WebShop), TRIAGE consistently improves over the GRPO baseline (dashed line). Bar labels report mean success rate; vertical axes are truncated per panel to make differences visible.

top_papers.title: When LLMs Read Tables Carelessly: Measuring and Reducing Data Referencing Errors
top_papers.abstract: This paper systematically evaluates data referencing errors (DREs) in LLMs when processing tables, finding they occur across various models. The authors propose a critic-based approach to identify and filter these errors, significantly improving answer accuracy. They also train a lightweight critic model capable of detecting DREs, enhancing the reliability of LLM table reasoning.

top_papers.title: CoMet: Context and Multiplicity Decomposition for Multimodal Uncertainty Estimation
top_papers.abstract: CoMet addresses uncertainty estimation in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) by decomposing it into two key components: context-specific ambiguity and multiplicity of plausible answers. This method trains a lightweight post-hoc module to quantify these distinct sources of uncertainty, enabling MLLMs to better "know what they don't know" in complex, open-ended tasks.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the proposed multimodal uncertainty. Even for the same image x x , uncertainty could vary by the given context t t . We decompose the uncertainty of a multimodal input into two components: (1) Context-specific uncertainty quantifies how broadly the context t t defines the plausible answer space ( e.g. , “is that a calico cat?” induces two answers, “yes” and “no”, whereas “what letter of the alphabet is on the bottle?” induces 26 assuming the answer is in English) (2) Multiplicity-specific uncertainty quantifies how many context-induced plausible answers ( e.g. , “yes” and “no”) remain compatible with the observed input x x ( e.g. , the cat features are visible and clearly correspond to a tabby cat, and therefore “no” is the only plausible answer, whereas the bottles are small and not well-lit, thus increasing rather than reducing uncertainty)

top_papers.title: FedLAB: Traceable Semantic Codebooks for Federated Multimodal Graph Foundation Learning
top_papers.abstract: FedLAB addresses federated multimodal graph learning by introducing traceable semantic codebooks. These codebooks organize multimodal graph knowledge, allowing for joint representation learning while explicitly exposing how different modalities, node semantics, and topology contribute to predictions, all under strict data isolation. This enables both effective knowledge transfer and semantic traceability in decentralized settings.
top_papers.figure_caption: A traceable modality-evidence interface. FedLAB decomposes a multimodal prediction into text-side and image-side evidence codes rather than only producing a fused representation.

top_papers.title: Scalable Behaviour Cloning on Browser Using via Skill Distillation
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a method to enable scalable behavior cloning for browser agents by distilling user interaction trajectories into compact, natural-language "skills." These skills are then organized into a skill graph, allowing agents to retrieve, reuse, and compose them. The core contribution is a scalable approach to leverage implicit human knowledge in web browsing for agent decision-making, addressing the bottleneck of incomplete information.
top_papers.figure_caption: Motivation of BrowserBC. BrowserBC turns human browsing traces into reusable natural-language skills, providing agents with decision priors for navigating unfamiliar websites.

top_papers.title: Adversarial Pragmatics for AI Safety Evaluation: A Benchmark for Instruction Conflict, Embedded Commands, and Policy Ambiguity
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces "adversarial pragmatics" as a novel benchmark and annotation protocol for evaluating AI safety. It addresses limitations of existing benchmarks by providing a linguistically controlled taxonomy and metadata to distinguish failures arising from instruction conflict, embedded commands, and policy ambiguity, rather than just simple pass/fail labels. The core contribution is a more nuanced and robust method for assessing AI behavior in complex, ambiguous natural language scenarios.
top_papers.figure_caption: Evaluation pipeline for the benchmark artifact. The diagram summarizes the planned data flow from pre-specified item metadata through model output, rule-aided triage, expert labels, LLM-judge labels, adjudication, and metric-driven item revision. No performance quantity is encoded in the figure.

top_papers.title: Agentic generation of verifiable rules for deterministic, self-expanding reaction classification
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a multi-agent LLM framework that automatically generates verifiable reaction classification rules from chemical patent data. The system
top_papers.figure_caption: a. The multi-agent LLM framework for dynamic taxonomy expansion. Raw reaction data and a seed hierarchy are evaluated by sequential high-level and detailed classification agents. A verifier agent audits the proposed labels; when existing categories are insufficient or imprecise, a label generation agent proposes new classifications. A taxonomy updater integrates these proposals, dynamically expanding the hierarchy to capture unrepresented chemistry without human intervention, resulting in an expanded reaction taxonomy. b. The iterative refinement loop for reaction template generation. An LLM drafts initial, broad SMIRKS patterns (low specificity, high recall) which undergo automated validation against reaction data. Through successive iterations, the LLM applies chemically grounded structural mutations to minimize false positives while maximizing true positive recall, yielding a robust database of generalized, high-specificity reaction templates.

top_papers.title: AutoMem: Automated Learning of Memory as a Cognitive Skill
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces AutoMem, a framework that treats memory management in LLMs as a trainable cognitive skill,
top_papers.figure_caption: Memory skill optimization with Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct . Starting from a base agent equipped with file-system memory (v0), AutoMem progressively improves performance through memory scaffold optimization (v0–v5/v4/v2), followed by memory proficiency training (+train) that yields further gains on top of the optimized scaffold.

top_papers.title: CausalMix: Data Mixture as Causal Inference for Language Model Training
top_papers.abstract: CausalMix reframes LLM data mixing as a causal inference problem, treating data features as covariates and domain mixture as treatment. This allows it to estimate the optimal mixture for larger datasets and models by extrapolating from smaller experiments, overcoming the need for costly retraining when data distributions shift. The core contribution is a scalable and robust method for data mixing that leverages causal modeling to improve LLM training efficiency and performance.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the CausalMix pipeline. Historical proxy runs provide data-state covariates, mixture assignments, and downstream outcomes, which are used to estimate state-conditioned marginal data returns through orthogonal causal learning.

top_papers.title: Cheap Code, Costly Judgment: A Case Study on Governable Agentic Software Engineering
top_papers.abstract: This paper explores how generative AI is transforming software engineering by shifting the focus from implementation effort to managing AI-generated code. The core method is a 12-week case study where an expert engineer used AI coding agents to build a system, meticulously documenting the process. The key contribution is a proposed theory of "governance conversion," a process model explaining how to

top_papers.title: Diffusion-GR2: Diffusion Generative Reasoning Re-ranker
top_papers.abstract: Diffusion-GR2 addresses the inference speed limitations of generative reasoning recommenders by adapting autoregressive models to a block-diffusion framework. It introduces novel techniques to overcome accuracy gaps arising from parallel decoding, specifically by ensuring valid ranking structures and mitigating off-policy learning issues during fine-tuning. This allows for significantly faster recommendation generation without sacrificing accuracy.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of Diffusion-GR2. We start from the AR GR2 reasoning re-ranker, initialize a block-diffusion decoder from its weights, and recover the conversion gap through conversion fine-tuning (CFT), on-policy distillation (OPD), and reinforcement learning (RL). The final model preserves the GR2 re-ranking interface while reducing sequential decoding cost through block-parallel denoising.

top_papers.title: Skills Are Not Islands: Measuring Dependency and Risk in Agent Skill Supply Chains
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Agent Skill Supply Chains (ASSCs) to address the implicit dependencies within LLM agent skills. Their core method, SkillDepAnalyzer, extracts natural language evidence to model skills as dependency-bearing artifacts, creating dependency graphs. This approach accurately recovers skill metadata and dependencies, outperforming existing baselines and contributing a novel framework for managing and understanding the complex interdependencies in agent skill ecosystems.
top_papers.figure_caption: SDA generation workflow.

top_papers.title: Towards Developing a Multimodal Chat Assistant for University Stakeholders: RAG-based Approach
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a multimodal university chatbot using a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approach. It combines a large language model with semantic retrieval from university resources to answer complex, domain-specific queries, accepting both text and image inputs. The contribution lies in creating an intelligent, adaptable, and deployable assistant for university stakeholders, particularly in resource-constrained environments.
top_papers.figure_caption: Architecture of our developed system.

top_papers.title: GSRQ: Gain-Shape Residual Quantization for Sub-1-bit KV Cache
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses the memory bottleneck of LLMs' KV caches by proposing Gain-Shape Residual Quantization (GSRQ). GSRQ introduces a novel "Gain-Shape K-means" (GSKM) algorithm that improves directional preservation during quantization, unlike traditional methods that can lead to centroid shrinkage. This allows for more efficient sub-1-bit compression of the KV cache, enabling larger context windows.
top_papers.figure_caption: Illustration of a hypothesized vicious cycle in high-dimensional ℓ 2 \( \ell_{2} \) -based K-Means. Averaging cancellation induces centroid shrinkage, which reduces angular sensitivity and leads to poorer direction and assignment, further reinforcing cancellation.

top_papers.title: Is One Layer Enough? Training A Single Transformer Layer Can Match Full-Parameter RL Training
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how reinforcement learning (RL) adaptation is distributed across transformer layers in large language models. The core finding is that training just a single transformer layer can recover most, and sometimes even exceed, the performance gains achieved by full-parameter RL training. This challenges the common assumption of uniform layer contribution and introduces "layer contribution" as a metric to quantify this phenomenon.
top_papers.figure_caption: (a) Layer contribution (defined in § 2.2 ) across all seven models studied in this work, plotted against depth-normalized relative layer position (0 = Layer 0, 1 = Layer L − 1 L{-}1 ). Each point corresponds to RL training performed on a single transformer layer while all remaining layers are frozen. The y-axis measures the fraction of full-parameter RL improvement recovered by training that layer alone. Solid lines denote models with full layer scans; dashed lines denote models where a representative subset of layers is trained. Despite spanning two model families, three RL algorithms, and two task domains (mathematical reasoning and agentic tasks), all seven models exhibit the same structure: layers around 40%–60% of network depth consistently achieve the highest contribution, with some surpassing full-parameter RL training (green-shaded region). (b) Performance comparison on the three Qwen3 models (NuminaMath-CoT, math benchmarks). Best Guided denotes the best result among the layer contribution-guided strategies from § 4 : either boosting the learning rate of high-contribution layers or selectively training only those layers. The percentage annotations indicate additional gain beyond full-parameter RL, expressed relative to the total RL gain. Training only these high-contribution layers consistently outperforms full-parameter RL across all three scales.

top_papers.title: Message Passing Enables Efficient Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Message Passing Language Models (MPLMs), a framework that allows LLM threads to communicate directly via send/receive primitives, unlike traditional fork-join methods. This direct communication reduces redundant context sharing and enables preemption, allowing threads to terminate early based on peer information. The core contribution is an efficient scaling mechanism for LLM reasoning by overcoming the limitations of transient, non-communicating threads.

top_papers.title: TiRex-2: Generalizing TiRex to Multivariate Data and Streaming
top_papers.abstract: TiRex-2 is a recurrent xLSTM-based foundation model that extends univariate time series forecasting to multivariate scenarios with past and future covariates. Its core method employs a memory-centric recurrent design with constant per-patch cost, overcoming the quadratic complexity and recomputation issues of Transformer models in streaming settings. This enables efficient integration of future-known covariates while maintaining causality, a novel combination for time series foundation models.
top_papers.figure_caption: Comparison of TiRex, Chronos-2, and TiRex-2. Chronos-2 supports multivariate forecasting with covariates, but is neither target-causal nor constant-memory. TiRex-2 adds these properties while preserving native multivariate covariate support (for more details see Table ˜ 2 ).

top_papers.title: ZO-Act: Efficient Zeroth-Order Fine-Tuning via One-Shot Activation-Informed Low-Rank Subspaces
top_papers.abstract: ZO-Act enables efficient fine-tuning of large language models without backpropagation by restricting perturbations to a low-rank subspace derived from input activations. This method optimizes lightweight coefficient matrices, reducing variance and enabling compatibility with standard optimizers, while naturally supporting quantized LLM fine-tuning by freezing low-bit weights.
top_papers.figure_caption: Stability and gradient alignment of activation-informed subspaces at epoch 3 of Llama-3-8B RTE full fine-tuning. We show the q-projection layers at decoders 0, 15, and 31.

top_papers.title: AGC-Bench: Measuring Artificial General Creativity
top_papers.abstract: AGC-Bench is a novel benchmark designed to measure artificial general creativity in LLMs, addressing the lack of unified evaluation in this domain. It systematically aggregates existing AI creativity benchmarks and standardizes them using an agentic harness. A key contribution is the development of AGC-Judge, an LLM fine-tuned using Judge Response Theory to mitigate bias in creativity scoring, enabling robust evaluation across diverse tasks.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overall and per-domain rankings. Left: top 15 of 83 models on the AGC-Bench composite. Right: top 5 within each of the six domains. Per-domain lineups overlap substantially with the overall ranking but reorder at the margins (e.g., kimi-k2.5 on Problem Solving, claude-opus-4.6-fast on Figurative Language). Full 83-model leaderboard in Appendix F .

top_papers.title: ACID: Action Consistency via Inverse Dynamics for Planning with World Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces ACID, a planning method for embodied control that addresses the issue of unrealistic intermediate transitions in world model rollouts. ACID enforces "cycle action consistency" by using an inverse dynamics model to ensure that backward-predicted actions match forward-predicted actions. This novel cost function improves planning performance and efficiency across various tasks and world models.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overall architecture of ACID. ( Left ): Decision-time planning with an action-conditioned world model: an MPC with CEM searches over candidate action sequences a 0 : H − 1 a_{0:H-1} to minimize the augmented planning cost. The current observation o 0 o_{0} is encoded by E θ E_{\( \theta \)} to z 0 z_{0} , and the world model F θ F_{\( \theta \)} unrolls a latent trajectory z ^ 1 : H \( \hat{z}_{1:H} \) from ( z 0 , a 0 : H − 1 ) (z_{0},a_{0:H-1}) . The inverse dynamics model G ϕ G_{\( \phi \)} then takes each predicted transition ( z ^ t , z ^ t + 1 ) (\( \hat{z}_{t} \),\( \hat{z}_{t+1} \)) and infers the action a ^ t \( \hat{a}_{t} \) that would explain it. ( Right ): The augmented planning cost combines two terms: the goal cost , which prefers candidate action sequences whose predicted final latent is close to the goal, and the action consistency cost , which prefers candidate action sequences whose predicted trajectory is realizable in environment.

top_papers.title: DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation
top_papers.abstract: DemoPSD addresses limitations in on-policy self-distillation for LLMs by introducing "selective adoption of teacher guidance." Instead of directly mimicking the teacher's dense supervision, it guides the student towards a "reverse-KL barycenter target," a weighted combination of teacher and student distributions. This approach balances learning from the teacher with the student's own exploration, mitigating overfitting and privileged information leakage.

top_papers.title: Fast Multi-dimensional Refusal Subspaces via RFM-AGOP
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces RFM-AGOP, a fast method for identifying multi-dimensional "refusal subspaces" in LLMs, which represent complex behaviors like refusing harmful queries. By adapting the efficient Recursive Feature Machine algorithm with a probe-informed initialization, it significantly speeds up subspace extraction, making it practical for reasoning models. The contribution lies in achieving both computational efficiency and improved performance in identifying these critical behavioral subspaces.

top_papers.title: Neuron-Aware Data Selection for Annotation-Free LLM Self-Distillation
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Neuron-Aware Data Selection for Annotation-Free LLM Self-Distillation (Neuron-OPSD). Its core method uses internal neuron activations to intelligently select training data and construct a teacher model for self-distillation. This approach aims to improve LLM performance in specialized domains without costly human annotations, overcoming limitations of previous annotation-free methods.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the Proposed Neuron-OPSD.

top_papers.title: Online Safety Monitoring for LLMs
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a simple, real-time online safety monitor for LLMs. It leverages an external verifier model's signal and calibrates a threshold based on risk control to trigger alarms when safety is compromised. The core contribution is demonstrating that this straightforward thresholding approach is competitive with more complex sequential testing methods for detecting unsafe LLM outputs.
top_papers.figure_caption: Monitoring factuality on mathematical reasoning (MATH): CRC and UCB calibrate a single threshold to turn a PRM score into an alarm, yet detect incorrect answers earlier ( third row ) than e-valuators, which train several density estimators.

top_papers.title: Reasoning effort, not tool access, buys first-try reliability in agentic code generation: an observational study
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether increased capabilities or reasoning effort improves agentic code generation. Through an observational study of 90 agent runs building the same application, they found that **model generation (capability tier) was the primary driver of first-try reliability, not tool access or prompt design.** Their contribution lies in demonstrating that **frontier models significantly outperform older ones, and that specific complex tasks like container deployment remain challenging and inconsistently reliable across different agent configurations.**

top_papers.title: Steerability via constraints: a substrate for scalable oversight of coding agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a "constrained substrate" approach for scalable oversight of coding agents, drawing parallels to traditional software engineering practices like access control and coding conventions. By imposing these constraints, the system significantly improves the ability of a small reviewer agent to detect security vulnerabilities (backdoors) in a Python codebase, demonstrating a substantial increase in recall compared to unconstrained agents. The core contribution lies in showing that established engineering principles are a cost-effective and scalable method for managing AI coding agents, outperforming more complex agentic scaffolding.

top_papers.title: VisionAId: An Offline-First Multimodal Android Assistant for People with Visual Impairment, Featuring Personalized Object Retrieval
top_papers.abstract: VisionAId is an Android app that transforms a smartphone into an offline-first visual assistant for the visually impaired. Its core method integrates multiple on-device deep learning models for real-time object recognition, depth estimation, and face detection, with an optional cloud LLM for enhanced descriptions. A key contribution is its few-shot learning capability, allowing users to personalize object retrieval by simply photographing their belongings.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overall layered architecture of VisionAId. All perception runs on-device; the cloud language model is an optional component used only for narrative scene description and object labeling.

top_papers.title: WorldSample: Closed-loop Real-robot RL with World Modelling
top_papers.abstract: WorldSample addresses the high cost of real-world reinforcement learning (RL) for robots by creating a closed loop between physical interactions and a learned world model. It uses real robot data to train a high-fidelity world model, which then generates synthetic transitions to augment training. This approach, combined with a novel Policy-Paced Learning strategy for sample selection, significantly reduces the need for costly physical rollouts while improving policy performance.
top_papers.figure_caption: WorldSample overview. WorldSample expands real-robot rollout into multiple physically grounded synthetic trajectories with an action-conditioned world model, forming a real-synthetic training loop that improves success rate and reduces interaction cost.

top_papers.title: Bringing Agentic Search to Earth Observation Data Discovery
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces an agentic search system that leverages NASA's Earth Observation Knowledge Graph to improve discovery of geoscience data and tools. By combining a neural scorer fine-tuned on a new benchmark with BM25 and a zero-shot agentic reranking stage, the system significantly enhances search relevance and recall for natural language queries. The core contribution is demonstrating how agentic search amplifies the value of knowledge graphs for complex data discovery.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the three-stage agentic search pipeline (Section 5.1 ). Stage 1 : the Router first attempts to resolve each query via NASA official tools (Harmony, SDE, WorldView, Giovanni); queries that can be fully answered here terminate early. Stage 2 : if no official tool suffices, the hybrid BM25 + NN-SSC retriever surfaces the most relevant datasets from the NASA CMR corpus. Stage 3 : retrieved candidates are reranked by an LLM that autonomously invokes web search and arXiv paper lookup to ground its ranking decisions in external context; the final ranked list is returned to the user.

top_papers.title: DecompRL: Solving Harder Problems by Learning Modular Code Generation
top_papers.abstract: DecompRL addresses the limitations of LLMs in solving complex problems by learning to decompose them into smaller, independently solvable sub-functions. Instead of brute-force sampling or relying solely on gradient signals, this RL algorithm explicitly learns to generate and recombine modular code structures. This approach significantly expands the search space of potential solutions by combining implementations of different modules, enabling LLMs to tackle problems previously beyond their capabilities.
top_papers.figure_caption: Decomp RL solves harder problems than existing RL methods. Our hierarchical training learns 1) a decomposition policy that breaks down problems into simpler functions and 2) an implementation policy that writes code for each function in parallel. Given a high token budget, Decomp RL solves up to 35 % 35\% problems of the LiveCodeBench hard subset ( 2024 / 08 / 01 2024/08/01 to 2025 / 02 / 01 2025/02/01 ) Jain et al. ( 2024a ) .

top_papers.title: Transformer Geometry Observatory TGO-II: Representational Similarity Observatory
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces TGO-II, a framework for analyzing the geometric evolution of Vision Transformer representations during training. Using methods like CKA and SVCCA, it reveals that representational similarity decreases as training progresses, indicating increasing specialization of internal features. This work contributes a novel approach to understanding the internal dynamics of Vision Transformers beyond just attention mechanisms.

top_papers.title: WattGPU: Predicting Inference Power and Latency on Unseen GPUs and LLMs
top_papers.abstract: WattGPU introduces two predictive models that estimate LLM inference power and latency using only publicly available LLM metadata and GPU specifications. This novel approach eliminates the need for hardware profiling, enabling accurate predictions for unseen GPUs and LLMs. The core contribution is a generalized, hardware-agnostic prediction system for optimizing LLM deployments.
top_papers.figure_caption: GPU selection is a key lever for energy savings in LLM inference. For deploying Llama 3.1 8B with a Service Level Agreement (SLA) of a mean ITL < 25 <25 ms, it is possible to reduce power draw by 43% by choosing an A30 instead of an H100. Data from the Watt Counts dataset showing only GPUs where the LLM fits in memory.

top_papers.title: The Future of NLP may not be at NLP Conferences: Scholarly Migration Patterns in Natural Language Processing
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates scholarly migration patterns in NLP, finding that the field's center of gravity is shifting away from traditional NLP conferences like ACL. The core method involves analyzing publication trends of both established and new authors, comparing pre- and post-LLM eras. The key contribution is demonstrating a significant migration of NLP research to general ML venues and newer "Findings" tracks, indicating a blurring of disciplinary boundaries due to LLM advancements.
top_papers.figure_caption: Per-year mean share of papers at each of the three tracked venue families per cohort author, renormalized to sum to one (topic-active cohort, N = 4 , 181 N=4{,}181 ). The cohort’s *ACL share holds near 76–80% through 2020 and then declines to 63% by 2025; General-ML rises monotonically from ∼ \( \sim \) 4% in 2015 to 31% in 2025, overtaking AI-Broad (which falls from 19% to 7%) around 2022–2023.

top_papers.title: Will Scaling Improve Social Simulation with LLMs?
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates if scaling up Large Language Models (LLMs) improves their fidelity in social simulations. The core method involves training and evaluating LLMs under varying compute budgets and parameter sizes across opinion modeling, behavioral simulation, and longitudinal forecasting. The key contribution is the surprising finding that **compute scaling significantly improves social simulation fidelity**, suggesting that current scaling trends are beneficial for this application.
top_papers.figure_caption: Compute Scaling Laws. We observe log-linear improvements in task loss on all three social science tasks after scaling compute alone with models trained on DCLM (Li et al. , 2024 ) from 10 18 10^{18} to 10 20 10^{20} FLOPs.
